claimed_contribution: The paper proposes Multimarket Membership Mapping (M4), presented as the first market-structure mapping framework that explicitly accommodates multimarket membership products by visualizing them in each submarket context on one map.

It introduces a multimarket membership transformation (MMT) that links overlapping clustering outputs to map projection by creating submarket-specific product instances and reallocating local and global relationships.
It applies bootstrapped neural product embedding to online search clickstreams and reports external survey evidence supporting the use of search co-consideration to infer competitive relationships.
context: The empirical application is the DSLR camera market, where products span standard, semi-professional, and professional tiers and compete within brand-linked camera systems. Competitive relationships are inferred from consumer searches on a major price comparison website.
design_and_data: The study builds a four-phase framework (input preparation, multimarket membership identification, multimarket membership transformation, and map projection) using SpeakEasy overlapping clustering and t-SNE projection. Competitive relationships are inferred from clickstreams of 57,144 consumers (Q1 2016) searching 111 DSLR cameras from nine brands, using bootstrapped word2vec skip-gram embeddings and aggregation of pairwise relationship weights. The paper includes a numerical example and an empirical method comparison against MDS, VOS, DRMABS, and plain t-SNE, plus robustness checks via 10-fold cross-validation. It also reports a simulation study over 2,400 synthetic market structures (12,000 maps total) and a 500-respondent survey for external validity of the search-based inference premise.
main_findings: In the DSLR market, M4 identified 10 submarkets and 9 multimarket membership cameras (8% of 111 cameras), and these MMPs affected 50% of submarkets.

Compared with MDS, VOS, DRMABS, and plain t-SNE, M4 achieved better representation of local and global competitive structure in the empirical comparison, and simulation results showed statistically significant gains that were larger when MMP prevalence was higher.

For identified MMP cameras, most consumers searched within one or the other associated submarket rather than transitioning across submarkets (less than 25% switched), and the map revealed mismatches between some manufacturer tier positionings and consumer-perceived competitive contexts.
one_line_summary: The paper introduces M4, a mapping framework that detects and visualizes products competing across multiple submarkets, and shows with DSLR search data that accounting for multimarket memberships yields more accurate market-structure maps than common alternatives.
relation_to_prior_work: introduces a mapping approach that integrates overlapping clustering with map projection to handle multimarket memberships that prior market-structure maps typically miss because they assign one location per product. It also adds robustness-oriented implementation choices (including modularity filtering and bootstrap embedding aggregation) and provides external validation of a key search-based inference premise.
research_gap: Existing market-structure mapping methods generally place each product at a single map location, which can misrepresent products that consumers view as substitutes in multiple submarkets. The paper argues that prior approaches did not provide a single-map method that automatically detects and visualizes these multimarket memberships without requiring a priori assignment assumptions.
research_question: How can market-structure maps be constructed so products that compete in more than one submarket are identified and visualized in each local competitive context within a single map? Does explicitly modeling these multimarket memberships improve the accuracy and interpretability of local and global market-structure visualization relative to existing methods?
